title: SQL Injection in Product Search
description: User-controlled query parameter `req.query.q` is directly interpolated into a raw SQL string: `SELECT * FROM Products WHERE ((name LIKE '%${criteria}%' OR description LIKE '%${criteria}%') AND deletedAt IS NULL)`. No parameterization or escaping is applied, allowing attackers to execute arbitrary SQL. This can lead to full database compromise including data exfiltration and manipulation.

title: SQL Injection Authentication Bypass in Login
description: The login route directly concatenates `req.body.email` into a SQL query: `SELECT * FROM Users WHERE email = '${req.body.email || ''}' AND password = '...'`. An attacker can supply payloads like `' OR 1=1--` to bypass authentication entirely. This is a textbook authentication bypass via SQL injection.

title: NoSQL Injection via $where in Order Tracking
description: The order ID from `req.params.id` is directly interpolated into a MongoDB `$where` clause: `db.ordersCollection.find({ $where: 'this.orderId === \"${id}\"' })`. The `$where` operator executes arbitrary JavaScript server-side, enabling NoSQL injection and remote code execution within the MongoDB context. Attackers can enumerate all orders or cause denial of service.

title: NoSQL Injection via $where in Product Reviews
description: Product ID is concatenated directly into a MongoDB `$where` query: `db.reviewsCollection.find({ $where: 'this.product == ' + id })`. This allows injection of arbitrary JavaScript executed server-side by MongoDB. An attacker can extract all reviews regardless of product or perform server-side JavaScript attacks.

title: Remote Code Execution via eval() in User Profile
description: User-supplied username data is passed directly to Node.js `eval()`: `username = eval(code)`. This allows any authenticated user to execute arbitrary JavaScript on the server by crafting a malicious username. Full server compromise including file system access and network pivoting is achievable.

title: XXE via libxml with noent:true in File Upload
description: XML files are parsed with `libxml.parseXml()` with the `noent: true` option enabled, which resolves XML external entities. An attacker can upload an XML file referencing a local file via `<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM 'file:///etc/passwd'>` to read arbitrary server files. This can also be used for SSRF or denial of service via billion-laughs attacks.

title: Unsafe YAML Deserialization Leading to RCE
description: Uploaded YAML files are parsed with `yaml.load()` without the `safe` option, allowing deserializing of arbitrary JavaScript objects. Combined with execution in a VM context, a crafted YAML payload can achieve arbitrary code execution on the server. This is a classic unsafe deserialization vulnerability in the Node.js ecosystem.

title: Hardcoded RSA Private Key for JWT Signing
description: An RSA private key used to sign JWT tokens is hardcoded directly in source code. Any developer with repository access or anyone who obtains the source can forge valid JWT tokens for any user. This completely breaks the integrity guarantee of the authentication system.

title: MD5 Used for Password Hashing
description: Passwords are hashed using MD5 via `crypto.createHash('md5').update(data).digest('hex')`. MD5 is cryptographically broken, non-salted, and trivially reversible via rainbow tables or GPU cracking. All stored user passwords are at risk of offline cracking if the database is compromised.

title: Hardcoded HMAC Secret
description: A hardcoded secret `pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj` is used as the HMAC key: `crypto.createHmac('sha256', 'pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj')`. This secret is visible to anyone with source access and cannot be rotated without a code change. HMAC signatures using this key can be forged by any party with knowledge of the secret.

title: JWT None Algorithm Vulnerability
description: JWT verification does not explicitly restrict the `none` algorithm, allowing an attacker to craft unsigned tokens by setting `alg: none` in the header. This completely bypasses signature verification, enabling authentication as any user. An attacker can forge admin tokens without knowing any secret.

title: Weak Cookie Signing Secret
description: The cookie parser is initialized with the trivially guessable secret `kekse`: `cookieParser('kekse')`. This weak secret allows attackers to forge signed cookies, potentially bypassing session integrity checks. The secret is also hardcoded, preventing rotation without a code deployment.

title: Server-Side Template Injection via eval() in Username
description: Usernames containing the pattern `#{...}` are detected and the inner content is executed via `eval()`. An attacker who registers with a username like `#{process.mainModule.require('child_process').exec('id')}` achieves server-side code execution. This is a critical SSTI leading to RCE that requires only account registration.

title: Encryption Keys Directory Exposed with Directory Listing
description: The `/encryptionkeys` directory is served as a static file server with directory listing enabled. This exposes the JWT RSA private key and other cryptographic material to unauthenticated users. Any visitor can browse to `/encryptionkeys/` and download all key material, enabling JWT forgery.

title: Path Traversal in Log File Server
description: The log file download endpoint checks only for forward slash `/` characters but does not prevent other traversal sequences or encoded variants. An attacker supplying a filename with `..` or URL-encoded traversal sequences can read arbitrary files outside the log directory. Sensitive configuration files and credentials may be exposed.

title: Path Traversal in Key Server
description: The key server route validates against `/` but does not guard against other path traversal techniques or encoding bypasses. Combined with the `/encryptionkeys` directory being served statically, attackers can potentially access files outside the intended directory. Private key material could be exfiltrated.

title: eval() Used in Captcha Expression Evaluation
description: Mathematical captcha expressions are evaluated using `eval(expression)` where `expression` is dynamically constructed. If any user-controlled data influences the expression string, this becomes a code injection vector. Even without direct injection, using `eval()` for arithmetic is an unnecessary risk that establishes a dangerous pattern.

title: FTP Directory Exposed with Directory Listing
description: The `/ftp` directory is served as static content with directory listing enabled. This exposes internal documents and files to unauthenticated users. Files in this directory may contain sensitive business data, configuration, or backup files.

title: Overly Permissive CORS Configuration
description: CORS is configured with `cors()` and no origin restrictions, allowing any domain to make cross-origin requests including with credentials. This enables malicious websites to perform authenticated API calls on behalf of logged-in users. Combined with other vulnerabilities, this significantly expands the attack surface.

title: XSS Protection Header Disabled
description: The Helmet.js `xssFilter()` middleware is explicitly commented out: `// app.use(helmet.xssFilter())`. This removes the `X-XSS-Protection` response header that provides defense-in-depth against reflected XSS in older browsers. While modern browsers rely on CSP, removing this header unnecessarily reduces protection for legacy clients.

title: Support Logs Directory with Directory Listing
description: The `/support/logs` directory is served statically with directory listing enabled, exposing application log files to unauthenticated users. Log files typically contain stack traces, user activity, IP addresses, and sometimes credentials or tokens in query strings. This constitutes sensitive data exposure.

title: Weak CSP Allowing unsafe-eval
description: The Content Security Policy header includes `'unsafe-eval'` in `script-src`, which permits JavaScript's `eval()` and related functions in the browser. This weakens CSP's XSS protection significantly since injected scripts can use eval to bypass other restrictions. CSP with `unsafe-eval` provides substantially weaker protection than a strict CSP.

title: Outdated express-jwt with Known Vulnerabilities
description: The application uses `express-jwt: 0.1.3`, an extremely old version of the JWT middleware with known security vulnerabilities. Older versions of express-jwt did not properly reject tokens signed with the `none` algorithm. Upgrading to a current version is necessary to close known attack vectors.

title: Outdated sanitize-html with Bypass Vulnerabilities
description: The application uses `sanitize-html: 1.4.2`, an outdated version known to have XSS bypass vulnerabilities. Older versions could be bypassed using certain HTML constructs that were not properly sanitized. Any user input processed by this version may still contain executable XSS payloads.

title: Rate Limiting Bypass via Spoofable X-Forwarded-For
description: Rate limiting uses `X-Forwarded-For` as the client identifier, but this header is trivially spoofable by attackers. An attacker can rotate the value of this header on each request to completely bypass brute-force protection on the login endpoint. This renders rate limiting ineffective against targeted attacks.

title: Open Redirect via Substring-Matching Allowlist
description: Redirect URL validation uses `url.includes(allowedUrl)` substring matching rather than strict origin comparison. An attacker can craft a URL like `https://evil.com/https://github.com/juice-shop` that passes the allowlist check but redirects to a malicious domain. This enables phishing attacks via a trusted domain.

title: Insecure Deserialization in B2B Order Processing
description: B2B order data is processed using `safeEval` from the `notevil` library, which provides only partial sandboxing and has known escape techniques. Crafted payloads can break out of the notevil sandbox to execute arbitrary code. This is a lower-confidence finding as exploitation requires a known bypass for the specific notevil version in use.

title: IDOR in Basket Item Operations
description: Basket item modification checks the basket ownership via a comparison that may be bypassable using operator injection or type confusion. An authenticated attacker may be able to modify or delete basket items belonging to other users. The validation logic should use strict equality and verify ownership through the authenticated session rather than request parameters.

title: Local File Inclusion via Template Layout Parameter
description: The `layout` parameter from `req.body` is passed to `res.render()` without sufficient validation, allowing a malicious user to potentially include arbitrary Pug template files. While a blacklist check exists, blacklists are generally bypassable. An attacker could render unintended templates, leading to information disclosure or code execution.

title: Potential SSRF in Profile Image URL Upload
description: The profile image URL from `req.body.imageUrl` is fetched server-side after a regex-based SSRF check. Simple regex-based SSRF protections are frequently bypassable using DNS rebinding, IPv6 addresses, URL encoding, or redirect chains. If bypassed, the server can be used to probe internal services or exfiltrate metadata.

title: User Enumeration via Password Reset Error Messages
description: The password reset endpoint returns distinct error messages depending on whether the supplied email exists in the database. An attacker can enumerate valid email addresses by observing different responses. This aids targeted phishing and credential stuffing attacks against known-valid accounts.

title: No Password Complexity Requirements
description: Password validation only checks that the password field is non-empty, with no minimum length or complexity requirements enforced server-side. Users can set single-character passwords, making accounts trivially brute-forceable. This is compounded by the MD5 hashing (finding #9) which makes weak passwords even easier to crack offline.
